text: The surgeon performs a total laparoscopic hysterectomy, beginning with the control of the lateral attachment of the uterus. The round ligament is cut, and both ovaries are conserved. The salpingectomy is performed by removing the fallopian tube away from the ovary to preserve ovarian vascularization. The utero-ovarian ligament is controlled using a bipolar device and cut with a harmonic ace, creating a window to increase the distance between the ureter and the uterus.

The surgeon performs vesicovaginal dissection by pulling the uterus toward the sacrum and cranially retracting the bladder, progressively opening the vesicovaginal plane until reaching the anterior valve of the uterine manipulator. The anterior aspect of the vagina is clearly visible.

The surgeon controls the round ligament one centimeter outside the uterine cornea using the Harmonic Ace, which cuts the vascular ligament easily. The peritoneal dissection continues, followed by the left salpingectomy, where the mesosalpinx is cut two centimeters away from the ovary to preserve ovarian vascularization. The right utero-ovarian ligament is controlled with bipolar energy and cut. The dissection proceeds on the posterior leaf of the left broad ligament to reach the upper part of the uterosacral ligament, enhancing uterine mobility. The uterine pedicle, including the artery and vein, is skeletonized.

The surgeon applies bipolar energy to the uterine pedicle, ensuring control of the opposite side to reduce backflow on the right pedicle. The uterus is tracked to the left, and the peritoneum is opened. After skeletalization, bipolar energy is applied at the isthmic level, allowing for easy cutting of the uterine pedicle, starting with the artery followed by the vein. The procedure is bloodless, and the same steps are repeated on the left side, with the vein being cut medial to the artery.

The surgeon performs a colpotomy using a monopolar needle, starting with the anterior aspect of the vagina, then proceeding to the left lateral aspect. Both anterior and posterior uterine manipulator valves are clearly visible. The right lateral and posterior aspects of the vagina are then incised under visual control. The uterus is extracted vaginally, and hemostasis on the vaginal edges is achieved with Vicryl O stitches using the Rodol knot.

The surgeon places interrupted sutures to close the vaginal cuff, incorporating the utero-sacral ligaments to reduce the risk of post-operative vaginal prolapse. The final knot is tied, ensuring the vagina is completely closed. The right ureter is visually inspected, showing clear ureteral reptation, followed by inspection of the left ureter.

The surgeon performs an ovariopexy by passing a needle through the ovary and the right round ligament to fix the ovary, ensuring not to damage the external iliac vessel. This technique is used to decrease the risk of ovarian torsion during a total laparoscopic hysterectomy for an adenomyosis uterus.
